coredata.dc:title: Evolution of large-area reduced graphene oxide nanosheets from carbon dots via thermal treatment

coredata.dc:description: Herein, we reveal a novel and facile pathway for the synthesis of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) from annealed carbon dots (CDs). The CDs (~ 2.5 nm) were synthesized from L-ascorbic acid via microwave synthesis at 180°C, after which formation of rGO nanosheets (≥ 40% yield) was achieved through 10 min annealing of CDs under N2 from 250-700°C. The TEM images showed that the CDs are initially transformed into an accreted sphere-like turbostratic carbon (d = 0.3390 nm) after annealing at 250°C, and finally to a layered ‘graphene-like’ nanomaterial (length ≈ 0.2-2.6 µm) at thermal treatment temperatures ≥ 500°C. The transformation into rGO was evidenced by the decrease in the oxygenated functional group content and removal of the oxygen functional groups facilitated the growth of rGO nanosheets through the interconnection of the C-C bonds at the defected CD edges. The FTIR, PL, PDF, and 13C NMR spectra confirmed the loss of functional groups after annealing at 700°C. Raman, and in particular PDF data, confirmed and rationalised the growth and evolution of the structured carbon domains with increasing annealing temperature. A mechanism to rationalize the data is proposed.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the NRF, the University of the Witwatersrand (post graduate merit award to LMM) and the DSI-NRF Centre of Excellence in Catalysis (c*change). We would like to thank Dr Rudolph Erasmus from the School of Physics at University of the Witwatersrand for his assistance with Raman and photoluminescence collection and analysis. We would like to also thank Ms Rhandzu Rhikhotso for the high resolution TEM data, and Dr Werner Jordaan for XPS analysis (CSIR, Pretoria, South Africa). We are also thankful to Dr Memory Zimuwandeyi and Mr Mudzuli Maphupha (School of Chemistry at University of the Witwatersrand) for NMR data collection and data processing, respectively. We would also like to acknowledge Dr Cebisa Linganiso from the Microscopy and Microanalysis Unit (MMU) at the University of the Witwatersrand for AFM analysis. We thank Dr Graham King for the PDF measurements performed at the Canadian Light Source, a national research facility of the University of Saskatchewan, which is supported by the Canada Foundation for Innovation (CFI), the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council (NSERC), the National Research Council (NRC), the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR), the Government of Saskatchewan, and the University of Saskatchewan. Finally, sincere gratitude goes to Ms Petra Dinham from the School of Chemical and Metallurgical Engineering (CHMT), University of the Witwatersrand for the SEM analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we reveal a novel and facile pathway for the synthesis of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) from annealed carbon dots (CDs). The CDs (~ 2.5 nm) were synthesized from L-ascorbic acid via microwave synthesis at 180°C, after which formation of rGO nanosheets (≥ 40% yield) was achieved through 10 min annealing of CDs under N2 from 250-700°C. The TEM images showed that the CDs are initially transformed into an accreted sphere-like turbostratic carbon (d = 0.3390 nm) after annealing at 250°C, and finally to a layered ‘graphene-like’ nanomaterial (length ≈ 0.2-2.6 µm) at thermal treatment temperatures ≥ 500°C. The transformation into rGO was evidenced by the decrease in the oxygenated functional group content and removal of the oxygen functional groups facilitated the growth of rGO nanosheets through the interconnection of the C-C bonds at the defected CD edges. The FTIR, PL, PDF, and 13C NMR spectra confirmed the loss of functional groups after annealing at 700°C. Raman, and in particular PDF data, confirmed and rationalised the growth and evolution of the structured carbon domains with increasing annealing temperature. A mechanism to rationalize the data is proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which includes graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs), are an interesting new group of fluorescent nanomaterials that can be derived from many carbon-based precursors [1–4]. CDs are described as zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon nanostructures of < 10 nm in size. Their structural morphology is defined by the small number of layers of ordered and/or disordered graphene sheets with sp2/sp3 carbon terminal sites [4–6]. The differently hybridized carbon terminal sites enable modulation of the properties of CDs through the attachment of a variety of surface functional groups [4–8]. Generally, CDs have been found to have interesting properties such as tuneable fluorescence, good dispersibility, low toxicity, biocompatibility and extensive surface chemistry [4–7]. Owing to their diverse properties, CDs are of technological importance in a variety of fields and have recently become a widely studied carbon allotrope [4, 9]. As such, CDs have been exploited in a range of applications including biosensing and bioimaging [10–12], photocatalysis [13–15], electrocatalysis [15–17] and in optoelectronic devices [18, 19].

Due to the ever-growing demand for the use of CDs for next generation electronics, two generic synthesis approaches have been developed for the preparation of these carbon nanostructures [20, 21]. They are the “top-down” and the “bottom-up” approaches. The “top-down” approach involves breaking down large carbon structures such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon fibres and fullerenes to make CDs. The “bottom-up” approach involves building up small CDs from molecular sources such as ascorbic acid, carbohydrates and citric acid to large structures [20, 21]. The “bottom-up” methods are preferred since they are environmentally friendly, simple, cost effective and useful for large scale synthesis [3, 22]. Interestingly, reports have shown that CDs can be used to produce other carbon nanostructures such as graphene-based nanosheets (GNSs) via the modified “bottom up” process, however, these associated synthetic routes have rarely been described [23–26].

Graphene nanosheets (GNSs) that include pristine graphene, graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [27], are well-known for their extraordinary structural, mechanical, thermal, and optical characteristics and have been studied in the electronics [28, 29], biomedicine [30–34] and construction fields [35]. Furthermore, rGO plays a significant role as an intermediate product in the transformation of GO to pristine graphene. It also has properties similar to those of pristine graphene when compared to other GNSs [36, 37]. As such, rGO has been used as an alternative for pristine graphene formation due to the high cost associated with pristine graphene synthesis [36, 37]. Although GNSs have been known for many years, their synthesis can be challenging. The synthesis of GNSs includes the “top-down” approaches such as mechanical, chemical, thermal, electronical and solvent-/surfactant-based exfoliation of graphite, and the “bottom-up” growth from molecular substrates using the CVD method and graphitization of silicon carbide (SiC) [38–44]. Many of these syntheses approaches still suffer from drawbacks such as the use of expensive substrates and equipment as well as the use of high volumes of toxic solvents and surfactant molecules. There is therefore a need for other alternative methods for the production of GNSs using simple, cost effective and environmentally friendly methods [40].

In this study, CDs were synthesized by a simple single-step “bottom-up” microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using L-ascorbic acid as a carbon source. The CDs obtained were highly hygroscopic, due to the absorption of moisture by the surface functional group [45, 46]. During thermal deoxygenation of CDs, the CDs self-assembled to form rGO. The reaction products were monitored by a variety of characterization techniques (including microscopy and spectroscopy), and the data compared with literature reports detailing the synthesis of rGO from the chemical reduction of graphene. The product was also compared to a 3-D porous carbon framework (PCF) material produced by Hu et al. in which an acetone and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution was used to make the CDs [26]. Furthermore, a mechanism for the conversion was also proposed for the rGO produced in this study.

L-ascorbic acid (analytical grade) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, South Africa, and used as received. Distilled water was used as a solvent in the experiments. Ultra-high purity nitrogen gas (N2, 99.99 %) was supplied by Afrox, South Africa.

CDs were prepared using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction method reported by Gong et al. [47], but with modification. An Anton-Paar Multiwave 3000 SOLV microwave system with maximum power output of 1400 W and a controlled pressure of 60 bar was used. In a typical experimental reaction set-up, L-ascorbic acid (2.4 g) and 240 mL distilled water were mixed in a 500 mL beaker. Thereafter, 4 × 60 mL of this homogeneous reaction mixture was transferred into 4 × 100 mL microwave reaction vessels. The mixture was irradiated for 10 min at 600 W to attain an infrared temperature of 180°C. During synthesis, the colour of the solution changed from colourless to dark brown, indicating carbonization. The resulting solution was treated in a centrifuge at 15000 rpm for 15 min to separate the precipitates (large carbon agglomerates) from the supernatants. This precipitated material, made of aggregates of spheroidal carbons, was not further investigated. A TEM image of this product is given in Figure S1. Thereafter, the supernatants were filtered through a 0.22 µm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter. The purified CDs solution was placed in a rotary evaporator to remove water, and subsequently dried in an oven (60°C) for 3 days to obtain the CDs (28 % yield). The reaction was repeated multiple times until a desired amount of product was obtained.

For the thermal reaction studies, 0.5 g of the CDs were loaded into a quartz boat and annealed at different temperatures (200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700°C) in a CVD furnace for 10 min under N2 (100 sccm). After the annealing was completed, the furnace was cooled to room temperature in N2. In each case, the solid products obtained were crushed using a pestle and mortar to give a powdered layered carbon material. For simplicity, the samples will be referred to as T200, T250, T300, T400, T500, T600 and T700 for samples annealed at 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700°C, respectively. The un-annealed CDs will be referred to as pristine CDs (pCDs). The yields obtained for T200, T250, T300, T400, T500, T600 and T700 are shown in Table 1
below. After annealing, the samples were characterized.

Morphology studies were conducted using an FEI G2 TECNAI Spirit T12 transmission electron microscope (TEM), with electrons accelerated at 120 kV, and a JEOL JEM 2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) fitted with a LaB6 gun for high resolution imaging. A lacy carbon grid was used in the TEM studies. A Veeco Di-3100 atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used in tapping mode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis was performed using a ZEISS SIGMA 03–39 FESEM at 20 kV. The Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were collected (128 scans) using a Brucker TENSOR 27 FT-IR spectrometer, scanning between 4000 and 600 cm−1. 13C solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were measured using a Bruker-500 MHz NMR spectrometer at a spinning frequency of 62.5 kHz (4.0 µs, 115 W). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using a Physical Electronics Quantum 2000 equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα excitation source operating at 1486.7 eV.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were collected on a Bruker D2 Phaser X-ray diffractometer equipped with a CuKα1/2 radiation (λKα=0.15406 nm) source and a Lynxeye detector. Samples were scanned over the 2θ range from 10 - 90 ° 2θ using a 0.026 ° step size. Raman spectra and solid-state photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) data were collected using a Horiba LabRAM HR micro-Raman spectrometer (frequency double Lexel SHG argon ion laser). Silica was used as a substrate. Raman measurements were done with a 514.5 nm (green) laser and the PL measurements were done with a 244 nm excitation wavelength. Total scattering data were collected on the Brockhouse high-energy wiggler beamline at the Canadian Light Source using a wavelength of λ = 0.2081 Å and a PerkinElmer XRD1621 area detector placed 160 mm after the sample. The data were processed using GSAS-II [48]. The Qmax used to produce the PDF of the measured samples was 23.4 Å−1. The instrumental resolution parameters Qdamp and Qbroad, as defined in PDFgui software, were determined by fitting a Ni powder standard measurement. Further data analysis was done using the xPDFsuite [49].A PerkinElmer STA6000 analyser was used to monitor the thermal stability of the samples from 35 to 900°C at a heating rate of 10°C/min, in air at 10 sscm with N2 as the purge gas.

Carbonization of L-ascorbic acid to CDs using microwave-irradiation is amongst the most common synthetic methods used to make CDs in the “bottom-up” synthetic approach [22]. This method possesses environmentally friendly attributes such as direct heating and a relatively short synthesis time [50–52]. Pristine CDs (pCDs) tend to be hygroscopic, due to their hydrophilic functional groups [45, 46]. Despite the 3-day period used to dry this sample, it was still obtained as a gel-like solid. The pCDs were then subjected to annealing for 10 mins at temperatures between 200-700°C to obtain the products, T200-T700. The aim of this treatment was to remove the moisture and/or surface functional groups and obtain solid CDs. The pCDs before annealing were analysed using TEM and HRTEM. From Figure 1
a the TEM images revealed quasi-spherical CDs with average particle sizes of 2.5 ± 0.2 nm, and a very narrow particle size distribution range between 1 and 4 nm (see Figure 1
a and Figure S2a), comparable to those previously reported [47]. The HRTEM images in Figure S2b also indicated also indicated that the CDs exhibited lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.204 nm, similar to that of the (101) plane lattice of graphite [53, 54]. The CDs synthesized in this study were classified under the crystalline quasi-type CDs as defined by Verma et al. [55] due to their size, d-spacing and low thermal stability.

In order to study the effect of temperature on the CDs, the pCDs were then heated to 200 °C (T200, Figure S3). However, little change was noted in their morphology and the size of the nanoparticles as the TEM micrographs showed that the T200 samples were similar to the pCDs. When the pCDs were heated to 250 °C, a drastic change in the morphology of the CDs (T250) was observed. At this temperature, the pCDs accrete and formed quasi-spherical nanoparticles, as seen in Figure 1
b and S4a. The particle sizes of T250 could not be determined because the material was accreted. The material revealed lattice fringes, with a d-spacing of 0.3390 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of a turbostratic graphite [56]. On further heat treatment of the CDs from 300 °C to 700 °C, the TEM images of the T300-T700 showed that the pCDs were transformed into large area layered carbonaceous nanostructures (see Figure 1
c, 1
d, Figures S4b, c and S5a-c). In particular, the T300-T400 materials appeared to be multi-layered carbon nanomaterials stacked on top of each other, with some of the accreted quasi-spherical nanoparticles embedded in between the layers and/or within the layered material ( Figure 1
c),
Figure 5
a). Most importantly, the T500-T700 samples revealed that the quasi-spherical nanoparticles had fully developed into a layered material ( Figure 1
d and S5b, c). For example, the HRTEM micrographs of the T700 sample showed no separation of layers and no visible lattice fringes (Figure S4c). To determine the possibility that the layer formation was due to the presence of residual starting L-ascorbic acid precursor, a reaction at 700 °C was performed, either with L-ascorbic acid or with L-ascorbic acid mixed with pCDs (50/50) under the same reaction condition used to synthesize T700. The TEM images (Figure S5d, e) of the resulting product showed that the morphology was different from the large area layered carbonaceous nanostructures observed in T700. The results indicated that the L-ascorbic acid actually hindered the formation of the layered nanostructures from pCDs. The results showed that evolution of the layered nanostructured from pCDs was facilitated by the heat treatment of the pCDs through the removal of functional groups (see below). The sheet sizes of samples are reported in Table 1 . The data showed that the nanosheets produced in these experiments were fairly large, typically with length 0.2-2.6 µm, and did not vary significantly in size for temperatures > 400 °C.

It is interesting to compare these products with a related study by Hou et al. [26] in which CDs were produced from acetone/NaOH solutions. The authors also observed a conversion of CDs to sheet like carbons, but that the carbons did not form layered sheets but 3D porous carbon frameworks (PCFs).

The SEM images of T250, T400 and T700 are shown in Figure S6. The SEM images showed that the materials contained layered nanosheets. The T250 image (Figure S6a) showed a layered material with a rougher surface texture as compared to samples T400 and T700. This may be attributed to the accreted quasi-spherical structure that formed before the material was fully transformed into layers as observed in the TEM image ( Fig. 1
b). AFM surface topography profiles in Figure S7 confirmed the quasi-spherical nanoparticles embedded surfaces of the sheets, with loss of CDs at the higher temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in the functional group content
ce:section.ce:para:
FTIR analysis ( Fig. 2
a) was performed to gain insight into the type/number of surface functional groups that were removed from the pCDs during the formation of the layered materials. The data showed a broad band at 3287 cm−1 that indicated the presence of an O-H stretch, due to residual water in the sample, and also likely the presence of the hydroxyl-functional groups on the carbon surface [47, 57]. The low intensity bands at ~3000 cm−1 and between 2986 and 2872 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic and aliphatic C-H stretches, respectively. The band at 1739 cm−1, was ascribed to the C=C bending mode in aromatic rings. The band at 1610 cm−1 suggested the presence of the carboxylic acid, C=O group. The peaks between 1215 and 970 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric (alcohol C-O-C) and symmetric (ether C-O-C) stretching bands, respectively. After heat treatment, the O-H band for the T200-T700 samples disappeared, and this was evident from the formation of ‘dry’ solid samples. Moreover, the O-H, C=O, C-O-C bands decreased dramatically for T300-T500, signifying the removal of oxygen functional groups, while sample T600 and T700, showed little to no bands associated with any functional group.

To further understand the role of temperature on the surface functional groups present in these samples, 13C solid-state NMR spectra were recorded on the T200, T400 and T700 samples (see Fig. 2
b); these are the 3 samples that showed large changes in morphology with temperature. The spectra were deconvoluted and the contribution of each deconvolution peak to the overall spectrum is summarized in Table 2
. Note that the aromatic region (sp2 carbons) is observed as a broad peak indicating C atoms in a range of different chemical environments. Significant changes in functional groups with temperature were observed. For example, the T200 sample showed peaks at ca. δ 170, 75, and 67 ppm, corresponding to the O-C=O, C-OH and C-O-C, respectively [58]. These peaks were not observed in the T400 spectrum. This indicated that annealing removed the O-C=O, C-OH and C-O-C functional groups from the pCDs surface at the lower temperatures. Since the aliphatic CH2 and the CH3 content remained similar (48% to 40%), while the aromatic content increased (27% to 52%), this suggests that the major source of the increased aromatic content are from the carbons containing the functional groups. Interestingly, when the pCDs were further annealed to 700 °C, the only prominent broad peak was found at ca. δ 117 ppm, corresponding to sp2 aromatic C. The trend observed in these spectra showed some similarities to those observed when GO is converted to rGO [59].

In summary, the TEM, FT-IR, 13C NMR and XPS results show that there is a correlation between the reduction in oxygen functional groups on the surface of the pCDs that is related to the changes in morphology leading to the formation of the layered structures.

Powder XRD studies by Yadav et al. have shown that a reduction in oxygen functional groups can be accompanied by a shift of the GO peak from ca. 10 ° 2θ, to a graphitic (002) peak at ca. 26 ° 2θ for rGO [60]. This implies that when graphitization takes place, oxygen moieties are thermally removed from the carbon surface and this suggests that similar data should be seen as the CDs convert to rGO.

The transformation of the pCDs as a function of temperature were evaluated by recording PXRD patterns of the products, and the results are presented in Fig. 3
below. The pCDs PXRD pattern showed a broad feature ca. 18 ° 2θ that has previously been shown to be typical of amorphous carbon nanomaterials and is associated with the (002) peak of disordered graphite [61]. The same feature was found to have broadened in the T200 sample when compared to pCDs. The PXRD of the T300 sample showed further broadening of the feature. This was also observed in the T400 sample, and there was a shift in position of the feature from ca. 18 ° to ca. 21 ° 2θ. The shift in peak position coincided with the removal of oxygen functional groups, as seen in the FTIR, 13C NMR and XPS spectra [62] and also the change in morphology of the samples. In the case of the T500, T600 and T700 samples, the major peak shifted from ca. 21° to ca. 24 ° 2θ, while the appearance of an additional smaller broad feature at ca. 14 o 2θ was noted. The small peak at ca. 43 ° 2θ was also observed in these three samples. Prior literature examples have shown that the occurrence of these features and the shifts that are observed in the relative 2θ positions are due to the conversion of GO to rGO [62–66]. The small sharp peak at ca. 30 ° in T500-T700 samples has also been reported previously when GO is converted to rGO [64, 66], but its identity is not known. Moreover, the feature at ca. 14 ° 2θ is usually seen in rGO when it is synthesized by the reduction of GO and broadly corresponds to the graphite oxide (001) plane [64].

Raman spectra were measured to obtain further information about the structural properties of the pCDs and annealed samples. Carbon nanomaterials are mainly characterized by two distinct peaks, namely the G (~1600 cm−1) and D peak (1300 cm−1), as well as some minor peaks; the 2D (~2700 cm−1) and the D+G (~2900 cm−1) overtones [67–69]. The Raman spectra shown in Fig. 4
and Table S2 summarizes the peak positions as well as the ID/IG ratios of the material. In the case of pCDs and T200 samples, the Raman modes could not be characterized. This is associated with photoluminescence excitation of the carbon (see PL data in Figure S12) by the Raman radiation source. However, on heating the samples to 300°C and 400°C, the spectra for the T300 and T400 samples revealed the presence of a small G peak at ca. 1600 cm−1. The inclined baseline indicated that these materials still contained some fluorescence properties that are associated with the pCDs, from which the non-florescent carbons were formed, thus making quantification of the Raman spectra very difficult. However, the spectra of the T400 to T700 samples showed the D band (~1350 - 1380 cm−1), the G band (1590 - 1598 cm−1) and a 2D peak overlapping with the D+G overtones. Additionally, a band associated with diamond-like dangling bonds (*, ~1150 cm−1) was evident, an indication of growth of in-plane two dimensional structures. The first order Raman spectra were peak fitted to reveal the presence of more defect-sensitive bands such as the dangling diamond-like bonds (TPA, ~1204-1230 cm−1) as well as the vibrational modes corresponding to the presence of amorphous carbon domains (A band, ~1460-1501 cm−1), either around the graphitic carbon grain boundaries, or in between the layered carbon nanostructures [70, 71]. The values of the defect density ratios (ID/IG) were >1 (Table S2 and Table S3), an indication of the low degree of graphitization with increasing annealing temperature. This can be ascribed to the structural changes associated with the realignment of the carbon lattice upon formation of larger graphitic domains. However, the reduction in the ratio corresponding to the amount of the amorphous domains (IA/IG) with increased annealing temperature was suggestive of the improvement of the structure of the layered carbonaceous structures. This implied that the higher annealing temperature removed the amorphous carbons and facilitated the linking of the CDs to the defective sites on the layered carbonaceous structures. The linking and growth of bigger layered carbon domains was further shown by the increasing ratio of the dangling diamond-like carbons (ITPA/IG) to ~0.55. Moreover, interlinking of pCDs was observed to generate defective sites as seen by the relatively large values for the defect density per square centimetre (ND, Table S3) as well as the decrease in the sizes of the sp2 crystallites (La, Table S3) [68]. Additionally, a blue shift was observed in the graphitic G-band with increasing annealing temperature (Figure S9). These phenomena were attributed to the compressive strain on the C-C bonds with the carbon lattice due to the formation of more defects and/or the lateral growth of the carbon domains [44, 72].

Pair Distribution Function (PDF) experiments were conducted to examine the local structure of the pristine and annealed CDs. The resulting PDFs are shown in Figs. 5
and S10, and the fitting parameters in Figure S11 and Table S4. Figure S10 shows the PDFs of pristine CDs (CDs), and samples annealed at 250, 400 and 700 °C (i.e., T250, T400 and T700) to 40 Å. All of the PDFs, except the T400 sample, show no significant structural order past 10 Å corresponding to both intra and interlayer interactions. In the case of the pCDs and T250, the small domain sizes seen in the HRTEM would account for this lack of medium to longer range order. Sample T400 however, showed a marked increase in the structural coherence extending over the entire PDF range up to 70 Å. For T400, TEM images showed the material contained multi-layered carbons stacked on top of each other, with some of the accreted quasi-spherical nanoparticles embedded in between the layers and/or within the layered material resulting in greater 3D coherence with respect to the pCDs and T250 samples.

Fig. 5 shows a zoomed in region of the PDFs to 6 Å. This region of the PDF relates to the in plane carbon structure [73]. The increasing peak intensity (peaks A, B, and F) and reduction in the FWHM, with increase in annealing temperature, indicates an increase in the uniformity of the C-C bond distances. This is expected once the 1D sheets are formed and functional groups have been removed from the structure and with them, electron withdrawing oxygen groups. Further, as the graphene sheets are formed a reduction in strain occurs from a reduction in the curvature of the surface from the elimination of the spherical domains.

The PDF of pCDs, peaks B and E give information about the structure of the starting pCDs material. Peak B corresponds to sp3 carbon functional groups in a diamond type structure with a nearest neighbours bond length of 1.54 Å [74]. The broad shoulder occurring from peak A, extends to lower r and shows that some of the C-C bond lengths are reduced due to electron withdrawal from electronegative oxygen-containing functional groups. As these functional groups are removed as the annealing temperature increases, from 250 to 400 °C, the shoulder narrows and intensifies into peak A as the C-C distances become uniform as seen in peak A (sp2 C). Peak A corresponds to the C-C nearest neighbour distance in graphite for the C-C (sp2) bonds with d = 1.42 Å [73]. As the annealing temperature is increased, the tetragonally bonded sp3 C groups are converted to sp2 graphitic structures as shown by the conversion of peak B to A as the annealing temperature is increased to 250°C. Finally, peak E at 3.2 Å, noted in the pCDs, corresponds to the inter-layer atomic correlations, i.e. the lattice spacing of turbostratic graphite [56]. This peak reduces as the temperature is increased and as layered carbonaceous nanostructures are formed.

The peaks positioned at the real space distances of 1.41 (A), 2.48 (C) and 2.84 Å (D), respectively, exactly match the in-plane carbon-carbon bond distances in the aromatic-type ring of graphite [75]. The intensities of these three peaks increase as the annealing temperature increased from 250 to 700 °C, indicating an increase of the graphene phase order and coherence with increasing annealing temperature as well as a normalization of the bond distances once functional end groups were removed.

The third peak at 2.84 Å (D), is double the first C-C distance, and corresponds to the second, long diagonal in the carbon hexagon. If the carbon plane was buckled, this third peak would not be exactly twice the bond distance of the first one. However, this peak is broadened in the pristine sample showing that bond length distortions that occur may arise from the presence of oxygen containing electron withdrawing end groups that have not yet been eliminated. The G(r) peaks for A, C, D, and F are narrower (reduced FWHM) for T700 compared to T400 and are found at the expected graphite bond distances [76]. These peaks correspond to the in plane carbon structure, with reduced FWHM indicating less disorder and therefore less defects, for T700. This is expected as the structure grows and rearranges itself once functional groups are removed under annealing conditions.

A distinct feature of all three experimental PDFs for the annealed samples is that they have no peak at a real space distance of 3.4 - 3.5 Å where the interlayer atomic correlations in graphite are expected. Instead, the fourth peak (F) in the experimental PDFs appears at 3.75 Å, which is the subsequent in-plane C-C bond distance in graphite [75]. This is expected as the quasi nano-spherical domains are converted into 1D sheet like structures where no interlayer atomic correlation are expected.

These results further illustrate how the surface oxygen functional groups affect the structure of the produced nanosheets. The data suggests that the nanosheets are formed by the interlinking and growth of the carbon domains accompanied by the removal of surface oxygen functional groups. This process can be referred to as the “bottom-up” synthesis of rGO from pCDs. One might expect the interlinking of carbons to form C-C bonds would remove defects in the carbon planes; however, these defects were not totally removed. A similar effect was observed in the rGO produced via the conventional “top-down” synthesis, i.e. from graphite to GO to rGO. The thermal reduction of GO also produced rGO with a defected surface [60].

Changes in the optical properties of the samples as a function of thermal annealing were studied using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, as shown in Figure S12. The pCDs and T200 samples showed a peak in the near IR region with a peak maximum at 631 nm [77]. The peak intensity was significantly reduced as the temperature was increased from 200°C to 250 and 300°C. For temperatures > 300°C (sample T400-T700), the photoluminescence excitation was completely lost. This was be ascribed to the removal of the surface functional groups that played a significant role in the PL excitations of the materials.

Numerous research reports have indicated that graphitic carbon decomposes in air to give CO and CO2 at temperatures above 700°C, while carbonaceous materials with amorphous properties and/or containing large amounts of defects start to decompose at temperatures as low as 500°C [78, 79]. Additionally, the removal of the strongly anchored surface functional groups are shown by the decomposition peak at ~200°C in thermograms [80–82]. The thermogravimetric profiles of the samples were recorded. Fig. 6
displays the first order derivative thermograms, whilst the thermal stability profiles are shown in Figure S13. The thermogram of the pCDs showed that the material started to decompose between ca. 200 - 300°C, due to the loss of the oxygen functional groups [83]. The ~10 % weight loss at ca. 337°C was associated with the loss of strongly bound functional groups on the carbon skeleton [83]. Finally, the ~ 37 % weight loss at ca. 509°C was attributed to the loss of the carbon skeleton, indicating that the pCDs were only stable up to this temperature. For the T200 sample, the profile showed ~ 28 % and ~ 16 % loss at ca. 161°C and 365°C, respectively, associated with the loss of the surface functional groups. The sample lost its core (~ 56 % loss) at ca. 479°C.

In the T250 sample, the functional groups start decomposing at ca 281°C (~19 % weight loss). At ca. 466°C and 530°C, there was an ~ 81 % weight loss which may be associated with the loss of strongly bound functional groups and the carbon core. Similarly, the profile of the T300 sample showed an initial weight loss of ~17 % at ca. 329°C associated with loss of the surface functional groups, and the major weight loss between ca. 436 and 504°C could be attributed to the remaining functional groups and the carbon core. Additionally, the DTG profile for the T400 sample showed a major decomposition peak at 432°C, with ~ 99 % weight loss, and a small peak at ca. 542°C which signified the complete decomposition of the carbon core. This data agrees with what was observed from the FTIR, 13C NMR and XPS data which showed that the sample still contained functional groups; however, the improved thermal stability further suggested that most of the surface functional groups had been removed. Interestingly, for the T500, T600 and T700 samples only one major decomposition peak at ca. 422, 430 and 450°C, respectively, was observed. The results obtained for the annealed samples (T400-T700) are comparable with the TGA results of rGO found in the literature, in which the TGA profile of the defective rGO exhibited a thermal stability up to 480°C in air [84].

The data presented here provides a facile route to the synthesis of a layered carbon material like rGO. Furthermore, the characterization data has provided evidence for a method that can be used to study the assembly of carbon atoms in small CDs into generate large sheets of a graphitic like material.

We can speculate as to the mechanism by which this occurs (see Fig. 7
below). The CDs are comprised of graphitic layers (dmax = 2.5 nm, see Figure S3b) capped with functional groups. In the first step, removal of the functional groups occurred and at 400 °C and the carbon atoms were mainly sp2/sp3 type (13C NMR and XPS data). PDF data also confirms the formation of C-C bonds with increasing annealing temperature. However, as shown by PXRD data, this is not the final structure of the material even though the carbon has aromatic character (Raman and TGA data). The TEM data ( Fig. 1
c) indicated that CDs that form these sheets were initially embedded in the sheets themselves. Further heating completed the process to form large graphitic sheets.

The carbon growth process can only occur via two processes: i) an Ostwald ripening in which the carbons agglomerated via the gas phase or ii) a solid phase reaction involving reaction of CD edges. Fig. 4
b suggest that the CDs merge to form a flake. In this remarkable reaction the carbons edges react together to form the large flakes. C-C bonds are thus being formed at T = 400 °C. This temperature is similar to that used to produce carbon-carbon bond formation/cleavage. The mechanism is different from a study by Hou et al. [26] in which carbon sheets were also prepared from CDs. In this study the role of Na+ ions were shown to be important; the C-C bond separation distances were much larger and the carbons did not form a layered structure.

Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) has been synthesized from a novel “bottom-up” method using carbon dots (CDs) as precursors. The results showed that the conversion of CDs to rGO involved a loss of the hydroxyl-, ether-, carboxylic- and carbonyl- surface functional groups of the CDs at T > 400°C. This conversion suggested that the residual carbon atoms in the CDs assemble to form layers that are comprised of an rGO like material. The PDF data allowed for a comprehensive study of the changes of C-C bonds with annealing, data shown to be consistent with the TEM images . Raman and PDF data suggested that the rGO was most likely highly defective. This synthetic method presented a facile pathway for generating rGO using a relatively inexpensive method. Furthermore, it is clear that this method opens up a simple pathway to doping rGO and that catalysts could be used to further reduce the temperature of the C-C bond knitting process.
